# Judging Criteria and Pitch Preparation

Category: Judging Criteria
Source: internal-hackbot-guide

Common judging criteria:

- Innovation: the idea is novel, creative, or applies technology in a fresh way.
- Technical complexity: the team built meaningful engineering work, not only a static mockup.
- Impact: the project solves a real problem for a clear user group.
- Presentation: the team explains the problem, solution, demo, and value clearly.
- Feasibility: the project can realistically be continued after the hackathon.

How to improve scores:

- Connect the project to a specific user and problem.
- Demo the most important workflow, not every feature.
- Explain what was built during the hackathon.
- Mention technical challenges and how the team solved them.
- Show measurable impact or a realistic adoption path.

Pitch deck structure:

- Slide 1: project name, tagline, team.
- Slide 2: problem and target user.
- Slide 3: solution overview.
- Slide 4: live demo or screenshots.
- Slide 5: technical architecture.
- Slide 6: impact, market, or user value.
- Slide 7: challenges, learnings, and future scope.
- Slide 8: ask, closing, or thank you.

Demo tips:

- Use a stable demo path.
- Prepare seed data before presenting.
- Keep backup screenshots or a short demo video.
- Assign one person to drive the demo and one person to narrate.
- Avoid logging into services live unless necessary.

Judge Q&A preparation:

- Be ready to explain why the problem matters.
- Know what is real, mocked, or planned.
- Explain your stack choices.
- Mention privacy, security, and scalability when relevant.
- Answer honestly and briefly, then return to impact.

# Hackathon Platforms

## Devpost
Category: Hackathon Platforms
Source: https://devpost.com/hackathons

Devpost is a major hackathon discovery and hosting platform for online, in-person, student, corporate, and themed hackathons. It is useful for finding beginner-friendly events, prize tracks, rules, judging criteria, submission requirements, and project galleries.

Use Devpost when a participant wants broad hackathon discovery, project inspiration, submission examples, or a place to host a project portfolio.

Official link: https://devpost.com/hackathons

## Major League Hacking (MLH)
Category: Hackathon Platforms
Source: https://mlh.io

MLH supports student and community hackathons, especially university-focused events. It is useful for beginners because MLH events often provide workshops, mentors, starter resources, community support, and structured event calendars.

Use MLH when a student wants a beginner-friendly hackathon path, campus events, or community-based learning.

Official link: https://mlh.io

## ETHGlobal
Category: Hackathon Platforms
Source: https://ethglobal.com

ETHGlobal runs Ethereum and Web3-focused hackathons, summits, and builder events. It is best for blockchain, smart contract, wallet, DeFi, DAO, NFT, ZK, and decentralized application projects.

Use ETHGlobal when a team wants Web3 mentors, sponsor prizes, protocol-specific tracks, and serious blockchain builders.

Official link: https://ethglobal.com

## Hack2Skill
Category: Hackathon Platforms
Source: https://hack2skill.com

Hack2Skill hosts hackathons, innovation challenges, corporate challenges, and developer competitions. It is useful for students and professionals looking for structured problem statements, industry themes, and challenge-based events.

Use Hack2Skill when a participant wants theme-based challenges, corporate innovation events, or India-friendly hackathon discovery.

Official link: https://hack2skill.com

## DoraHacks
Category: Hackathon Platforms
Source: https://dorahacks.io

DoraHacks is a global hacker movement and platform for hackathons, bounties, grants, and Web3 ecosystem events. It is strong for blockchain communities, open-source ecosystems, and project funding opportunities.

Use DoraHacks when a team wants Web3 bounties, ecosystem grants, blockchain hackathons, or ongoing funding opportunities.

Official link: https://dorahacks.io

## Unstop
Category: Hackathon Platforms
Source: https://unstop.com

Unstop hosts hackathons, case competitions, hiring challenges, quizzes, and student competitions. It is popular for college events, corporate competitions, and career-linked challenges.

Use Unstop when a student wants hackathons connected to colleges, companies, career opportunities, and structured competition rounds.

Official link: https://unstop.com

# Hackathon Project Ideas

Category: Project Ideas
Source: internal-hackbot-guide

## AI

- AI study mentor that turns course notes into quizzes, summaries, and personalized revision plans.
- Resume and interview coach that scores answers and suggests role-specific improvements.
- Smart meeting assistant that extracts actions, risks, owners, and deadlines.
- Local language health assistant that explains basic symptoms and recommends professional care when needed.

## Web3

- Donation transparency dashboard using wallet transactions and milestone proofs.
- DAO proposal summarizer that explains governance proposals in plain language.
- Credential wallet for hackathon certificates, badges, and verifiable achievements.
- Micro-grant platform for open-source contributors.

## Healthcare

- Appointment triage assistant for clinics that routes patients by urgency and department.
- Medicine reminder and caregiver alert app.
- AI-powered report explainer that turns medical reports into patient-friendly summaries.
- Rural telehealth intake system with multilingual support.

## FinTech

- Expense anomaly detector for students and freelancers.
- Financial literacy coach that explains savings, taxes, and credit basics.
- Fraud pattern dashboard for small merchants.
- Subscription tracker that identifies unused recurring payments.

## Smart Cities

- Citizen issue reporting app with location, photo evidence, and prioritization.
- Public transport crowding predictor.
- Waste pickup optimization dashboard.
- Flood or heat risk alert system using public data.

## Education

- Personalized learning path generator.
- Peer study group matcher.
- Assignment feedback assistant.
- Teacher dashboard that identifies struggling students from quiz patterns.

Strong hackathon ideas usually have a clear user, a painful problem, a small demoable MVP, measurable impact, and a story judges can understand in under one minute.

# Team Formation

Category: Team Formation
Source: internal-hackbot-guide

Good hackathon teams usually have 3 to 5 members. A solo project can work for small events, but a balanced team usually moves faster because design, backend, frontend, pitching, and testing happen in parallel.

Recommended team roles:

- Product lead: defines the problem, user, scope, and final story.
- Frontend developer: builds the user interface, user flows, responsiveness, and demo polish.
- Backend developer: builds APIs, database models, integrations, authentication, and deployment.
- AI or domain specialist: handles model usage, data, prompts, evaluation, or subject-matter logic.
- Designer or presenter: improves UX, slides, demo script, storytelling, and judge Q&A.

Responsibilities:

- Decide the problem statement in the first 10 to 20 percent of the event.
- Keep the MVP small and demoable.
- Assign one owner for the final pitch.
- Maintain a shared task board.
- Freeze core scope before the final few hours.
- Prepare fallback screenshots or a local demo video.

Team size guidance:

- 1 member: best for a focused prototype or learning project.
- 2 members: good for frontend plus backend or prototype plus pitch.
- 3 members: strong minimum for a serious hackathon submission.
- 4 members: ideal for product, frontend, backend, and AI/design split.
- 5 members: useful for larger builds, but coordination becomes more important.

# Tech Stack Selection

Category: Tech Stacks
Source: internal-hackbot-guide

## React

React is a strong frontend choice for interactive dashboards, chat interfaces, admin panels, and hackathon demos. Use it with Vite for fast setup and fast iteration.

## Node.js

Node.js with Express is useful for REST APIs, authentication, integrations, webhooks, and connecting frontend apps to AI APIs or databases.

## FastAPI

FastAPI is a good backend choice for Python-heavy projects, AI services, data processing, and ML model APIs. It provides automatic API docs and works well with Python AI libraries.

## Firebase

Firebase is useful for fast authentication, realtime databases, hosting, and serverless functions. It is a strong hackathon choice when speed matters more than custom infrastructure.

## MongoDB

MongoDB is useful for flexible JSON-like data, chat histories, user profiles, submissions, and rapidly changing schemas.

## PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL is best when data relationships, reporting, transactions, and reliability matter. Use it for structured apps, analytics, and production-ready relational data.

## Supabase

Supabase gives PostgreSQL, authentication, storage, edge functions, and realtime features. It is a strong alternative to Firebase when teams want SQL plus a fast backend platform.

Recommended hackathon stacks:

- AI assistant: React, Node.js or FastAPI, Groq/OpenAI-compatible API, MongoDB or PostgreSQL, vector store.
- Web3 app: React, Node.js, wallet integration, Solidity, Hardhat, Ethers or Viem, IPFS if needed.
- Healthcare MVP: React, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, secure auth, careful disclaimers, audit-friendly logs.
- FinTech MVP: React, Node.js, PostgreSQL, strong validation, role-based access, clear risk messaging.
- Education app: React, Firebase or Supabase, AI API, simple analytics dashboard.
